author: @mamupelu565
text:
I heard toothpaste is a good thermal paste, you should test that as sort of a control.
Also, you should subtract the room temperature from the measurements

author: @cb253
text: I'm currently conducting tests using the conductive ink to protect the all aluminum ice giant cooler from reacting with the gallium based liquid metal. The ink did protect the aluminum in a test. I haven't gotten thermal baselines yet. Also suprised to see how well the liquid metal mixed with the conductive ink. I also haven't tested this, to determine if it increased or decreased the thermal transfer properties. But I'm hopeful.

author: @blaknyte
text: I would suggest a test rig that could be run for longer periods.  Set up a heater block with some electrical heat source that can stay consistent.  A couple of heat probes run through an Arduino to log the results over time.  With that kind of setup, you could run multiple tests and add new tests as they come to mind.  As was suggested below, different brands of thermal paste against your ink and your new ink paste.  But anyway, good job on the videos and the experiments.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Like you said, it needs to be fair. How long was the original thermal paste in the laptop for compared to the fresh new paste you made for a difference of only 4 degrees C. I have to ask if 4 degrees will make a difference on the life of the laptop at temperatures between 85-95 C.

author: @William_Hada
text: I really like the results from this test and the use of the ink as a heat conductor, but I think I should be run over a longer length of time to be valid. Temperature versus time should be looked at. Checking it after weeks or months should be done.

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: Fumed silica is a desiccant, so I believe he's using the glycerol to help balance that somewhat.  He mentions he's using it as an "anti-drying agent".

author: @juhotuho10
text:
Did you check if the CPU downclocked at any point in the test? 
Laptops are designed to downclock the chip when it's about to overheat in order to manage the heat production and prevent unwanted device shutdowns and hardware failure.

Also some manufacturers do cheap out on thermal paste and this could also impact the original thermal performance.

What you have made seems very promising but it would have been better to see a comparison against a freshly applied commercial paste instead of unknown old thermal compound

author: @martinX74
text: Nice test. Keep on the good work Luke 👍🇩🇰 Just wondering if you cleaned out dust from the fan between the tests🤔

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
